lessons.title: Volatile Memory: SRAM vs DRAM and DDR
lessons.content:
The memory hierarchy trades speed for density and cost, and the first split is **volatile vs non-volatile**: volatile memory loses its contents when power drops. Two volatile families dominate.

**SRAM** stores one bit in a **6-transistor (6T) cell**: two cross-coupled inverters form a bistable latch, plus two access transistors gated by the **word line** that connect the cell to a differential **bit-line** pair (BL, BL'). Because the latch actively holds its state, SRAM needs **no refresh**, has a **non-destructive read**, and is very fast. The price is area: six transistors per bit means **low density** and high cost. That is exactly why SRAM is used where speed matters and capacity is modest, CPU caches and **FPGA block RAM**.

**DRAM** stores a bit as charge on a single capacitor, the **1T1C cell**: one access transistor plus one tiny capacitor. This is enormously dense and cheap, but the capacitor **leaks**, so every row must be periodically rewritten, the **refresh** requirement (typically every ~64 ms). Reading drains the capacitor, so the read is **destructive** and the sense amplifiers must **write the value back** after every access. DRAM is addressed in two phases, **row** then **column** (RAS/CAS), to share pins.

Modern **SDRAM/DDR** is synchronous to a clock and organized into **banks** so accesses can be interleaved. **Bursts** stream several words per command, and **CAS latency (CL)** is the clock-cycle delay from a read command to first data. DDR transfers on **both clock edges** (double data rate); successive generations (DDR2/3/4/5) raise bus rates and burst length while lowering voltage.

items.front: SRAM 6T cell vs DRAM 1T1C cell: structure and the key consequences of each.
items.back: Left, SRAM: two cross-coupled inverters (a bistable latch) plus two access transistors to a differential bit-line pair, gated by the word line. It actively holds state, so no refresh and non-destructive read, but six transistors per bit means low density. Right, DRAM: one transistor and one storage capacitor. Very dense and cheap, but the charge leaks (needs refresh) and reading discharges the cap (destructive read, requires write-back).

items.front: Why does DRAM need refresh while SRAM does not?
items.back: DRAM stores each bit as charge on a tiny capacitor (1T1C). Junction and subthreshold leakage drain that charge over milliseconds, so each row must be periodically read and rewritten (refresh, typically every ~64 ms) to keep the data valid. SRAM stores its bit in a cross-coupled inverter latch (6T) that is actively driven by the supply, so as long as power is present the state is regenerated continuously and no refresh is needed.
items.hint: Charge on a leaky cap vs an actively-driven latch.

items.front: What does 'destructive read' mean for DRAM, and how is it handled?
items.back: Sensing a DRAM cell connects its storage capacitor to the bit line, which shares/drains the cell's charge so the original value is lost (corrupted) by the act of reading. The sense amplifiers detect the small voltage swing, regenerate the full logic level, and write the value back into the row (precharge/restore) after every read. This is part of why DRAM has a row-cycle time and why a whole row is opened at once.
items.hint: Reading drains the cap; sense amps must write it back.

items.front: Why is DRAM addressed in two phases (RAS then CAS)?
items.back: To save package pins and cost, the address is multiplexed: the row address is presented first and latched by RAS (Row Address Strobe), opening an entire row into the sense amplifiers; then the column address is presented and latched by CAS (Column Address Strobe) to pick the word within that open row. This halves the number of address pins and matches DRAM's internal two-dimensional array organization.
items.hint: Pin saving; row strobe then column strobe.

items.front: In DDR SDRAM, what do 'double data rate', 'banks', 'burst', and 'CAS latency' each mean?
items.back: Double data rate: data is transferred on BOTH the rising and falling edges of the clock, doubling throughput for a given clock. Banks: independent sub-arrays that can have rows open simultaneously, so accesses interleave and hide row-open latency. Burst: one command streams several consecutive words (burst length) instead of one. CAS latency (CL): the number of clock cycles from issuing a read (column) command until the first data word appears.
items.hint: Both edges; interleaved arrays; multi-word streams; read-to-data delay.

items.prompt: A design needs a small, very fast on-chip scratchpad with single-cycle deterministic access and no controller overhead. Which memory fits best?
choices: SRAM (e.g., FPGA block RAM)
DDR4 DRAM
NAND flash
Serial EEPROM
items.explanation: SRAM gives fast, deterministic, non-destructive single-cycle access with no refresh and no multi-phase addressing, which is exactly what an on-chip scratchpad or cache wants. DRAM adds refresh, RAS/CAS, and controller latency; NAND and EEPROM are non-volatile but far slower and block/page oriented.

items.prompt: Why is DRAM able to achieve much higher bit density than SRAM?
choices: Its 1-transistor-1-capacitor cell is far smaller than SRAM's 6-transistor cell
DRAM cells store multiple bits each by design
DRAM does not need bit lines
SRAM cells require larger capacitors than DRAM
items.explanation: A DRAM cell is just one transistor and one (trench/stacked) capacitor, while an SRAM cell needs six transistors for its cross-coupled latch and access devices. The much smaller per-bit cell area is the entire reason DRAM is denser and cheaper per bit, at the cost of refresh and destructive reads.

items.prompt: On a DDR memory whose I/O bus runs at 1600 MT/s, what is the effective clock frequency of the data strobe driving it?
choices: 800 MHz
1600 MHz
3200 MHz
400 MHz
items.explanation: DDR transfers data on both clock edges, so the megatransfers-per-second figure is twice the clock frequency: 1600 MT/s corresponds to an 800 MHz clock. (Internal prefetch and core arrays run slower still; the 2x edge trick is what gives 'double data rate'.)

items.prompt: Which statement about CAS latency (CL) is correct?
choices: It is the number of clock cycles from a read command to the first valid data word
It is the time the DRAM can hold data without refresh
It is the number of banks in the device
It is the voltage margin on the bit lines
items.explanation: CAS latency is the delay, measured in clock cycles, between issuing the read (column-address) command and the appearance of the first data on the bus. It is a key DRAM timing spec; lower CL at a given data rate means lower read latency. Refresh interval, bank count, and bit-line margin are separate parameters.

lessons.title: Non-Volatile Memory: ROM, EEPROM, and NOR vs NAND Flash
lessons.content:
Non-volatile memory keeps data with power removed. The family tree runs from mask **ROM** (contents fixed at fabrication, cheapest at volume) through **PROM** (one-time fuse programming), **EPROM** (UV-erasable), to **EEPROM** (electrically erasable, byte-addressable read and write). EEPROM is ideal for small, frequently changed data such as calibration constants and serial numbers, but it is expensive per bit.

**Flash** is the dominant non-volatile technology. The storage element is a **floating-gate** (or charge-trap) transistor: charge trapped on an isolated gate shifts the cell's threshold voltage, and that shift is read as a 0 or 1. Flash can be programmed bit-by-bit toward one state but can only be **erased in large blocks**, hence **erase-before-write**: you cannot simply overwrite a byte; the block must be erased first.

Two architectures diverge sharply. **NOR flash** wires cells in parallel for fast random read and full address/data buses, so code can run directly from it, **Execute-In-Place (XIP)**, with no copy to RAM. It is used for boot code and firmware. **NAND flash** strings cells in series, giving far higher density but only **page/block** access (no random byte read), making it the basis of mass storage (SSDs, eMMC, SD cards).

Cells also trade endurance for density: **SLC** (1 bit/cell) is fastest and most durable; **MLC/TLC/QLC** pack 2/3/4 bits per cell at lower cost but with slower writes, weaker error margins, and fewer program/erase cycles. Because every erase wears the oxide, flash needs **wear leveling** (spreading writes across blocks), plus ECC and **bad-block management**, all handled by the flash controller/FTL.

items.front: Order the classic ROM family by programmability: ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM.
items.back: Mask ROM: contents defined at fabrication, not changeable, cheapest in high volume. PROM: one-time programmable by blowing fuses/antifuses in the field. EPROM: erasable by UV light through a quartz window, then reprogrammable (bulk erase). EEPROM: electrically erasable and writable in-circuit, byte-addressable for both read and write, but costly per bit. Each step adds reprogrammability at higher cost per bit.

items.front: How does a floating-gate flash cell store a bit?
items.back: The cell is a MOSFET with an extra electrically isolated 'floating' gate buried in the oxide between the control gate and channel. Injecting/removing charge on that floating gate (via tunneling or hot-electron injection) shifts the transistor's threshold voltage. When read, the cell either conducts or not at a given gate voltage, and that threshold shift is interpreted as the stored bit. The charge stays trapped with power off, giving non-volatility. (Modern devices may use a charge-trap layer instead of a conductive gate.)
items.hint: Trapped charge shifts the threshold voltage.

items.front: What is 'erase-before-write' in flash, and why does it exist?
items.back: Flash programming can only move bits in one direction (typically setting them to 0) cell-by-cell, but returning bits to the erased state (1) can only be done a whole block at a time. So you cannot overwrite arbitrary data in place: the block must first be erased (all 1s), then programmed. This block-granular erase is a consequence of the cell/array structure and is why flash needs a translation layer to update small amounts of data efficiently.
items.hint: Program bit-by-bit one way; erase only in big blocks.

items.front: NOR vs NAND flash: cell arrangement, access granularity, and typical use.
items.back: NOR: cells in parallel, full random-access read with address/data buses and fast read of any byte, so code can run directly from it (XIP). Lower density, used for boot/firmware. NAND: cells in series strings, much higher density and cheaper, but read/write only by page and erase by block (no true random byte read). Used for mass storage (SSD, eMMC, SD). NOR favors code execution; NAND favors bulk data.

items.front: What is Execute-In-Place (XIP) and which flash supports it?
items.back: XIP means the CPU fetches and executes instructions directly from the non-volatile memory without first copying them into RAM. It requires fast, random-access reads with a memory-mapped interface, which NOR flash provides. NAND flash cannot do true XIP because it lacks random byte read (page-oriented, needs a buffer), so NAND-based systems copy/boot code into RAM first.
items.hint: Run code straight from NOR; NAND must copy to RAM.

items.front: Compare SLC, MLC, TLC, and QLC flash on bits/cell, speed, endurance, and cost.
items.back: SLC = 1 bit/cell: fastest, highest endurance (most P/E cycles), most expensive per bit. MLC = 2 bits/cell, TLC = 3, QLC = 4: progressively higher density and lower cost per bit, but slower (more program steps and read levels), tighter voltage margins (more ECC needed), and fewer program/erase cycles before wear-out. More bits per cell trades durability and speed for capacity and cost.
items.hint: More bits/cell = cheaper/denser but slower and less durable.

items.prompt: A microcontroller must boot and run its firmware directly from non-volatile memory with no external RAM copy. Which is the natural choice?
choices: NOR flash, because it supports random-access reads and XIP
NAND flash, because it is densest
QLC NAND, for lowest cost per bit
DRAM, because it is fast
items.explanation: Execute-in-place requires fast random byte reads over a memory-mapped bus, which NOR flash provides. NAND (including QLC) is page-oriented and cannot do true XIP, so it must copy code to RAM first. DRAM is volatile, so it cannot hold boot code with power off.

items.prompt: Why can you not simply rewrite a single byte in NAND flash the way you can in EEPROM?
choices: NAND can program toward one state cell-by-cell but can only erase in large blocks, so an in-place byte change is impossible without erasing the whole block
NAND has no data bus
NAND bytes are read-only after manufacture
NAND requires UV light to write
items.explanation: Flash erase is block-granular: bits can be programmed one way per cell but only returned to the erased state a whole block at a time (erase-before-write). To change one byte, a controller must read-modify-write an entire block (or write to a fresh block and remap), which is why a flash translation layer exists. EEPROM, by contrast, supports true byte erase/write.

items.prompt: What problem does wear leveling solve in a flash-based storage device?
choices: It spreads program/erase cycles across all blocks so no single block wears out prematurely
It increases the read speed of the device
It removes the need for error-correcting codes
It eliminates the erase-before-write requirement
items.explanation: Each flash block tolerates only a limited number of program/erase cycles before the oxide degrades. Wear leveling (in the controller/FTL) distributes writes evenly across physical blocks so hot, frequently-updated logical data does not destroy a few blocks while others stay fresh, extending device lifetime. It does not change erase granularity or remove the need for ECC and bad-block handling.

items.prompt: For storing a few hundred bytes of calibration constants that are updated occasionally and must survive power loss, the best fit is:
choices: EEPROM (byte-erasable, in-circuit writable, non-volatile)
NAND flash with QLC cells
SRAM backed by nothing
Mask ROM
items.explanation: EEPROM is non-volatile and supports true byte-level erase/write in-circuit, ideal for small, occasionally-updated parameters like calibration data. NAND is block-erased and oversized for a few hundred bytes; SRAM is volatile (loses data on power-off); mask ROM cannot be updated at all.

lessons.title: Memory Organization, Dual-Port, and FIFOs
lessons.content:
A memory chip is specified as **depth x width**: depth is the number of addressable locations (set by the number of address lines, $2^n$), and width is the bits per location (the data-bus width). A 8Kx8 device has $2^{13}=8192$ words of 8 bits, so 13 address lines and 8 data lines, plus control lines (chip-select **CS**, output-enable **OE**, write-enable **WE**).

You build bigger memories from smaller chips two ways. To **widen** the word, place chips side by side sharing the same address and CS but driving different data-bus slices, two 8Kx4 parts make an 8Kx8. To **deepen** (add capacity), stack chips that share the address and data buses; the **high-order address bits** drive a **decoder** whose outputs are the per-chip **chip-selects**, so exactly one chip responds at a time. This **chip-select decoding** is how a 2:4 decoder lets four 8Kx8 chips form a 32Kx8 map, with A14-A13 selecting the bank and A12-A0 selecting the word inside it.

Key timing: **access time** is the delay from a valid address (or CS) to valid data; a read/write **cycle** also includes setup, hold, and recovery.

Standard single-port RAM allows one access per cycle. **Dual-port RAM** has two independent address/data ports, so two agents (e.g., two clock domains or a CPU and a DMA engine) can read/write the same array concurrently. A **FIFO** is built on dual-port memory with separate write and read pointers and full/empty flags; an **asynchronous FIFO** uses Gray-coded pointers synchronized across clock domains, the canonical safe **clock-domain crossing** structure from the timing module. **CAM** (content-addressable memory) inverts the model: you present data and it returns the matching address.

items.front: Depth x width expansion with a chip-select decoder: how four 8Kx8 chips form a 32Kx8 memory.
items.back: Each 8Kx8 chip uses the low address bits A12-A0 to pick a word inside it and shares the common 8-bit data bus. The high address bits A14-A13 feed a 2:4 decoder whose four outputs drive the four chips' chip-select pins, so exactly one bank is enabled for any address. Bank 0 covers 0x0000-0x1FFF, bank 1 0x2000-0x3FFF, and so on, giving a contiguous 32Kx8 map.

items.front: What do 'depth' and 'width' mean for a memory device, and how do they relate to pin counts?
items.back: Depth is the number of addressable locations, equal to 2^(number of address lines); width is the number of bits stored per location, equal to the number of data lines. A 16Kx8 part has depth 16384 (so 14 address lines, since 2^14 = 16384) and width 8 (8 data lines), plus control pins (CS, OE, WE). Depth x width is the device's total bit capacity.

items.front: How do you WIDEN a memory word versus DEEPEN total capacity using smaller chips?
items.back: Widen: place chips in parallel that share the same address lines and the same chip-select, but each drives a different slice of the data bus (e.g., two 8Kx4 chips -> 8Kx8). Deepen: stack chips sharing both the address and data buses, and use the high-order address bits through a decoder to generate a separate chip-select per chip, so only one responds per access (e.g., four 8Kx8 chips -> 32Kx8).
items.hint: Widen = parallel data slices; deepen = decode high bits to chip-selects.

items.front: What is the role of the chip-select (CS) line and why is a decoder used when deepening memory?
items.back: CS enables a memory chip; when deasserted the chip ignores the bus and tri-states its data outputs. When several chips share the same address and data buses to form a deeper memory, the high-order address bits are fed to a decoder whose outputs are the individual chip-selects. This guarantees exactly one chip drives the data bus for any address, preventing bus contention and mapping each chip to a distinct address range.
items.hint: CS enables one chip; decoder turns high address bits into one-hot selects.

items.front: Define memory 'access time' and how it differs from 'cycle time'.
items.back: Access time is the delay from presenting a valid address (or asserting CS/OE) until valid data appears on the outputs, it characterizes a single read. Cycle time is the minimum time before a new access can begin and includes access time plus setup, hold, precharge/recovery, and (in DRAM) write-back/restore. Cycle time is therefore equal to or longer than access time; the two differ most in DRAM where the destructive-read restore extends the cycle.
items.hint: Address-to-data delay vs minimum time between accesses.

items.front: What is a dual-port RAM and what is it used for?
items.back: A dual-port RAM is a memory array with two independent access ports, each with its own address, data, and control lines, allowing two agents to read/write concurrently (often in different clock domains). Uses: passing data between a CPU and a DMA engine or peripheral, sharing a buffer between two processors, and as the storage core of a FIFO. Designers must handle the case of both ports addressing the same cell simultaneously (collision/arbitration).
items.hint: Two independent ports into one array; concurrent access.

items.front: How is an asynchronous FIFO built, and why is it the standard clock-domain-crossing structure?
items.back: An async FIFO is a dual-port RAM with a write pointer in the write clock domain and a read pointer in the read clock domain, plus full/empty flag logic. The pointers are encoded in Gray code (only one bit changes per increment) and synchronized into the opposite domain through two-flop synchronizers; comparing the synchronized pointers safely generates full/empty. Gray coding ensures that even if a synchronizer samples mid-transition, the value is off by at most one count, never a wild value, making the crossing metastability-safe.
items.hint: Dual-port RAM + Gray-coded pointers synchronized across domains.

items.prompt: You need a 64Kx16 memory but only have 16Kx8 chips. How many chips and what addressing scheme?
choices: 8 chips: 2 in parallel for width (x16) and 4 deep banks selected by a 2:4 decoder on the high address bits
4 chips, all sharing one chip-select
16 chips with no decoder needed
2 chips: one for the high byte and one for the low address
items.explanation: Width: two 16Kx8 chips side-by-side give 16Kx16 (one drives D15-D8, the other D7-D0). Depth: 64K / 16K = 4 banks, so four such pairs = 8 chips total, with the top two address bits feeding a 2:4 decoder that drives the four bank chip-selects. That is 8 chips with a 2:4 decoder.

items.prompt: In a memory built from several chips on a shared data bus, what prevents two chips from driving the bus at once?
choices: A decoder ensures only one chip's chip-select is active per address, and unselected chips tri-state their outputs
Each chip drives a separate, private data bus
The data bus uses open-collector outputs only
The CPU reads all chips and votes on the result
items.explanation: Chip-select decoding maps each address to exactly one chip; a deselected chip places its data pins in high-impedance (tri-state), so it does not fight the active driver. This one-hot selection is what avoids bus contention on the shared data bus. The other options either defeat the purpose of sharing a bus or are not how standard SRAM/ROM buses work.

items.prompt: Why are FIFO pointers Gray-coded when the FIFO crosses clock domains?
choices: Only one bit changes per increment, so a synchronizer that samples mid-transition still reads a value off by at most one, never a corrupt multi-bit value
Gray code makes the FIFO deeper
Gray code removes the need for full and empty flags
Gray code lets both clocks run at exactly the same frequency
items.explanation: When a multi-bit pointer is sampled in another clock domain, several bits could be in transition at once with binary coding, yielding a meaningless value. Gray code changes only one bit per count, so a metastable sample resolves to either the old or new value, off by at most one, which keeps the full/empty comparison safe. It does not change depth, replace flags, or synchronize clock frequencies.
